sentences.text: My name is Dennis Urstein.
U-R-S-T-E-I-N.
I was born on February the 24, 1924, in Vienna, Austria.
Adolf.
Isn't it a lovely name?
God bless him.
I hope he drops dead if he's still alive.
I hope this is on tape, too.
Uh-- A-D-O-- A-D-O-L-F.
Yes.
It was shortened to Dolfi, D-O-L-F-I.
This was usually used for people that were called Adolf, and there were a lot of Adolfs around.
Yes.
Uh, I think it was David [?
Yitzhak, ?] as far as I can remember.
My father's name was Leon, but, uh, it's-- well, that's the name that he used, and that was the name on the passport, but I think his real name was, uh, Leibusch.
That's spelled L-E-I-B-U-S-C-H.
Marie.
And that was the same, M-A-R-I-E.
Brandriss, B-R-A-N-D-R-I-S-S.
Yes.
My father was born on, ah, May the 30, 1879, and my mother was born on January 6, ah, 1892.
I'll be 72 next February.
No.
I haven't got a clue.
I really don't know.
Yes.
They were-- no, no, Well, when you say from Vienna-- they lived in Vienna.
I think my father arrived in Vienna from-- [SIGH] Russia or Poland.
Poland, Russia, I know-- I, I don't know the geography down there.
And at the turn of the century, I think about 1899 or 1900 or 1901, I think my mother and her family came to Vienna-- later, I guess, 1905 or 1906.
I really-- I have-- I have no recollections.
I really don't know.
Uh-- German.
Austrian.
As a matter of fact, well, it was this way.
I spoke, ah, proper German at home and Viennese in the streets.
And my mother hated it.
Ah, she said, you're-- you're a bandit.
She said, you-- you can't talk like that.
But you know, as kids are-- that's the way we talked.
And I-- Viennese is a, a, very, very, ah, I don't know, a very nice, melodious-sounding ah, way of expressing oneself.
And so that's-- that's what I did.
Oh, yes.
My father, obviously, spoke Russian.
And he also spoke perfect Polish.
And, ah, my mother spoke Polish.
I think my mother was from-- from Poland, ah, from-- I remember that.
That-- that-- I know that name because my sister told me.
It was a place called [POLISH] Now, don't ask me to spell it because I haven't got a clue how to spell it.
That's where my mother came from.
My father was-- I don't know, uh, what, ah, city he was born in.
But, ah, very briefly, uh, the thing that I do know about his family was that, uh, they were intellectuals.
They were teachers.
They were music teachers.
They moved, ah, either before-- I don't-- don't remember, OK-- before the first World War or after the first World War to the area where Auschwitz was.
It was around Auschwitz.
Sosnowiec, Katowice, Bedzin.
That's the area they lived in, his family.
And-- and it was a big family.
I mean, his dad died, uh, when he was, uh, small-- I don't know if he was small or if he was grown up.
But my father was a black sheep, because where everybody was very studious, he was a vagabond, as they called it.
You know, he wanted to see the world, and that's why he-- he took off and went-- went to Vienna.
And that's what it is.
That's, that's all I know about him.
No, no.
None, none, none of them.
My, my mother's, yes.
Uh, my- my, uh-- the people on my mother's side, uh, was-- well, that's my all-time favorite.
That's my grandfather.
His name-- I don't-- even him I don't know.

It always gets me when I talk about him because he's, he's the guy that-- that I always say-- I told you last time that I'm not superstitious, but as far as my grandfather is concerned, he saved me every time there was a problem.
[CLICKS TONGUE] He was there for me.
He still is.
His name was Chune, C-H-U-N-E.
He died in Vienna when I was, I think, about, ah, let me see, now.
I think she said she was 12, and she's five-- so I must have been 5 or 6 years old.
And, uh, the memory I have, he was a very pious man.
See, my-- I'm not jumping, but I'm connecting.
Uh-- our household was very secular.
In other words, we didn't keep kosher.
I didn't even know what the hell kosher was, to be honest, until I almost came to this country.
And-- very secular.
We went to the synagogue to Yom Kippur and Rosh Hashanah.
And, I think-- oh, yes, just one thing I forgot.
Uh-- it just reminds me.
My mother, because of her upbringing, um, wanted to bring some religion into the-- into the household, more or less.
So I do remember that we didn't eat bread, ah, at the Passover.
And she had different dishes that [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, I forgot that.
You know such a-- oh, God.
Now I remember.
Well, there was a place where I lived in the, uh, 20th district of Vienna.
And the, uh, synagogue was-- I don't know the name, I know the street.
It was Kaschelgasse, K-A-S-C-H-E-L-G-A-S-S-E.
Uh-- I also sang in the choir down there because I had a very, very nice voice.
You know, we were a very, very singing family, not my father, but my mother, my sister.
My sister had a beautiful voice.
Lily, L-I-L-Y.
She's five years older than I am.
And, uh, I love her.
She's the only one I got.
She gives me hell every time we get together.
And we try to do that once a year.
But I love that kid.
Kid.
OK.
She's a 70 year-- 76 year old kid.
She's as stubborn as I am.
She lives in Germany, in Stuttgart.
Anyhow.
So one day, through singing, uh-- I don't know how it came about, but all of a sudden, I sang in the choir in the synagogue in the Kaschelgasse.
And, ah, I had a soprano, and I sang well.
I was given solos ah, on the Sabbath and especially Friday night.
There was something that we-- [INAUDIBLE] what it was like-- [SINGING IN HEBREW] --something like that.
And I sang that.
Oh, boy, I enjoyed it.
Ah, incidentally, the choirmaster was a lush.
He stank like a-- like, like a-- like a beer hall.
He was always drunk, and he used to hit us over the head.
That was part of it.
You didn't sing right, boom!
You got it over the head.
But we enjoyed it.
And [LAUGHS] I don't think we got paid for it.
But then I was discovered.
Some yo-yo came in and, ah, heard me sing on a Friday night.

And the next thing I knew, somebody came up to the house, introduced himself, and said that, uh, he would like to have me go to the main synagogue-- the synagogue in Vienna, which was-- is still in existence today.
It's called Seitenstettengasse.
Seitenstettengasse Temple.
Temple Seitenstettengasse-- Temple.
Don't ask me to spell it.
I forgot.
But everybody knows it because that's the only one that still exists today.
And I went there.
And I remember the choir master.
His name  was [?
Kurt ?] Fuchsbegelb, F-U-C-H-S-B-E-G-E-L-B.
Fuchsbegelb.
And he, ah, played-- he was a violinist and came from a very, very old, very distinguished, old Viennese Jewish family.
Uh-- his father had the title of [GERMAN].
No, that's something that only people that live down there will understand.
And again, don't ask me to spell that.
My spelling's lousy already.
And, uh, he was the choir master.
And, uh, I sang there.
I believe we had-- we had to-- had to meet once or twice a week to, uh, rehearse, and then we sang on Saturday, Friday evening, and Saturday morning.
And then we also had weddings.
And I got very well paid.
As a matter of fact, I-- I made more money after about six months than somebody that worked for a tailor shop or worked in a bakery as an apprentice.
They got peanuts, and I got, I remember, 25 shillings a month.
That was a fortune.
And--
When I went to Seitenstettengasse?
Let me see, now.
I think I must have been about either 10 or 11, something like that.
OK.
By 13-- I even had my bar mitzvah down there, which was quite an honor.
Well, everybody was there.
The family was there.
The, uh-- uh-- my uncles and my aunts and I--
Yeah, my mother's family.
Yes.
And, uh-- they were very thrilled and pleased.
And I even sang my, uh-- what was it called?
The, uh-- I forgot what the-- well, you, you-- you recite something at your bar mitzvah, the-- whatever it's called.
I forgot.
Sorry that-- you should know those things better than I do.
Um-- And it was a very pleasant, ah, morning.
And then we all went home.
We didn't hire a hall.
And we had, I think, about 15 or 20 people.
And I don't know how the heck they fitted in there, but they all did.
Also, oh, something very funny happened at the 13.
I was deflowered when I was 13 years old right at my--my bar mitzvah.
That's right.
That's it.
You don't know that.
I didn't tell you that.
See, you get the best out of me.
Uh-- that was the-- the superintendent's daughter.
I met her after I came back to Vienna, after the war.
She was the ugliest woman that you can even think of.
She was cross-eyed.
She had a long, pointed nose.
She had pimples all over the place.
And when I was 13 years old, she caught me while I was taking a-- a breath of fresh air in the corridor outside of our apartment.
And she took me down there, and she showed me, ah, everything about the birds and the bees.
I didn't-- I don't think that I performed very well because it was all new to me.
But that-- that's something I'd forgotten.
Boy, you're getting things out of me.
Incredible.
No!
She was the superintendent's daughter.
I don't even remember her name anymore.
Well, how could you?
Anyway--
Ah, my grandfather.
You know, my grandfather had-- I always said, and so did my sister-- like a candy store in his pocket.

Whenever anybody came to him-- and that was either my sister or myself or I had, I think, two cousins that were old enough from my mother's brother, Joseph, that also lived in Vienna-- always a candy.
I don't know where he got it from.
It was like a-- a-- a bottom that was never empty, you know, a bag that was never empty.
And what I can still smell is the-- the tobacco on his breath, or on his beard.
He had a small beard.
You'll see a picture of him, afterwards.
And I loved that man.
I don't know what-- you know, because he didn't speak-- he spoke a mixture of Yiddish and German, Austrian or German, you know, but I understood him.
And-- and before that, I never-- I never knew Jewish words or Yiddish.
I didn't know any of these things.
And, ah, I'm still in love with him, even today.
This is a great guy.
Yeah.
He was.
He still is.
When-- when I have a problem, I do not think about anything else, but I'm asking, I don't know, for guidance, help, mostly help, but anything.
You know, when, when, when my wife had a very serious operation about a year and a half ago, last January a year ago.
And while was she was, ah, in the operating room, and I knew that it was a serious operation, really serious, I talked to him.
I did that during the war.
I'm not jumping ahead.
I was selected once to go to the gas chamber or to be killed, let's put it this way, ah, because I-- I-- I-- I-- came out of having typhoid.
And I looked like-- well, I was what they used to call a mussulman.
You know, a mussulman was like two sticks instead of legs, and thin, and this and that, and dirty and filthy.
And um, he got me out of it.
I'll explain to you later how.
But, uh-- I mean how he did it not, but how it happened.
OK.
Well, one day we-- this was 19-- 1943.
Latter part of '43, I got typhoid, and I was in the hospital.
And I was [SIGHS] you know, what they used to call the K-- KB, Krankenbau.
Everything in, in the-- the camp was abbreviated-- Krankenbau.
Strafkompanie was SK.
And it was all abbreviations.
And uh-- a lot of my friends that knew me, that I worked with, that used to work in Kanada, used to bribe somebody on there to hide me underneath a bunk, uh, when there was a selection on.
There usually was one or two selections a week of people that-- you know.
And everybody stood straight up and said, well, I, Ich bin Gesund.
They looked like death warmed over.
So anyhow, I got out of this and, uh, and I, uh-- out of the-- out of the hospital.
And, uh, I worked in a place where I could sleep during the day under a big table that, uh, people sat around, uh, maybe, uh, uh, five, six meters in diameter.
And I slept there.
And that's how I got my strength back, but I still looked awful.
And, uh, one day, as we were on roll call in front of our barracks, all of a sudden, bingo.
You know, The selections weren't advertised.
They came about.
And, uh, here they came.
And the scribe was with the, uh, Kommandofuhrer who picked the people, or sometimes it was a doctor.
And, uh, they picked me.
And as soon as they write down your number on that sheet, the scribe, you had it, you know.
That-- that's it.
So, uh, we were all brought into a barrack.
I think it was barrack number seven in Birkenau.
And, uh, we were laying around down there.
This was about, oh, maybe 6:30 or 7:00 in the evening.
And, uh, that's it.
We were resigned.
That's it.
And that's when I went to the window.
[CRYING] Macho man, huh?
And I spoke to my grandfather.
Well, after that, there was a guy down there that had tools with him, who was also selected.
And, uh, I asked him to do something for me.
This was about, oh, maybe, and I'm strictly guessing, 10:00.
I said, uh, if I go, I'm going to go my way.
It was a stick with a-- it was like-- it was like-- the, the same type of tool that they used when they tattooed this thing into me, a stick with a needle on the end.
And he had a bottle there, something ink.
Who knows what?
And he put this thing on there.
I've got to show it to you later on.
It's a heart.
And it has four initials on there.
OK?
It says L for Liam, M for Marie, D for Dolfi, and L for Lily.
And that's the family.
And this happened, as I said, in the latter part of '43.
And I knew that my parents came and were gassed on the 22nd of April of 1943.
So it is-- it's very-- it's very faint, but you can still read it.
It says the 22nd of the fourth, 1943.
That's my grandfather.
Yeah.
Well, the story ended in a very, very peculiar way.
Uh-- usually, they, they brought the truck up around, uh, midnight and, uh, loaded everybody up there.
And away they went to either one, two, three, or five.
And, uh, then they were either-- usually, with, with small amounts of people like that, they weren't gassed.
They were shot in the back.
There was an assessment down there that-- that they caught after the war that did that.
And I forgot his name.
He came from Mannheim, I think.
Uh-- and then all of a sudden, somebody called my number.
And I run up to the person that did, which was a-- a-- a guy that is called a Stubendienst, which is the-- usually the flunky of the block elder that runs it.
He had people working under him that took care.
And he says-- I know the exact words in German-- [SPEAKING GERMAN].
In other words, excuse the expression-- you asshole, go back to your barracks.
And I went back to my barrack.
And I didn't know what happened.
There's a story behind all these things.
Uh-- but here I'm going to-- you wanted chronologically, and it isn't chronological, but it has to be said because it fits with it.
When I worked in Kanada in the Aufraumungskommando, which we will later on discuss in details, one day, a man showed up that worked in the same Kommando as a prisoner.
And he was an Unterkapo.
There was a Kapo, an Oberkapo, and an Unterkapo.
Then there were Vorarbeiters, which means-- wait a minute.
What the hell is a Vorarbeiter?
That, again, delegated people.
It was like a hierarchy from there to here to there.
And he came from Vienna.
But lo and behold, where all of us had a-- two triangles, a red and a yellow, next to our number, he had a green one on there, a green and a yellow.
So we know-- or knew immediately that he's of the-- let's say, he's a criminal, as we used to call them, OK?
Well, his name was Hans Schorr-- S-- he is not alive, so his name is really not important-- S-C-H-O-R or S-C-H-O-R-R.
Hans Schorr.
He was a real short stop, very wiry, very loud.
He used to use his stick a lot, tattooing you on the head or this and that.
But in-- and on the whole, he wasn't a bad guy.
He was just a nut.
You know?
And, uh, he got some of us together that spoke his language.
There were-- let me see, now.
There was [GERMAN], [?
Adi ?] [?
Wolfe ?], myself.
That's three.
And I think there was a fourth one.
No.
No, the other two were German boys-- that spoke the language and were in Kanada.
And he got us all together and said, OK, this is Hans' law.
He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
He said, when you find something that you can pocket in your hand, and it's gold, I want it.
And if you don't, they said, I'm going to rip your neck off.
One day, a transport came.
I think-- I believe that transport came from Holland.
That was in 1943, when I was still working down there.
And, uh, I found a gold coin in the, uh, wagon, you know, in the cars, the-- the-- the-- transport, the cattle cars where people were in.
And I looked at it.

And to us this was something that one could use in the camp-- I will tell you later-- because there were favors to be asked for if something happened to you, you needed somebody that you could turn to if-- and it happened to me.
It happened to many of us.
And we gave it to them when we came back, because we didn't use it.
We didn't need it in the Kanadakommando, as such.
And I pocketed it.
And he saw it.
And I-- you know, it was, it was like the cat watching the mouse.
He was just waiting-- what I'm going to do.
And I didn't do it.
I didn't move.
I kept it there.
He gave me such a beating.
I mean, he didn't kill me.
He didn't maim me.
But he gave me a beating that I felt for a long time.
This was Hans.
And this is a lesson to you, he said in Viennese.
[SPEAKING VIENNESE] If you do that one more-- one more time, I'll kill you.
OK.
Apparently, I found out, in 1944 this same Hans that behaved sometimes like an animal, that hit me very badly-- I have no scars from it, but very badly-- saved my life.
He found out that I was in there.
He bribed somebody on there, and they substituted my number.
He crossed my number off, said I died.
I don't know what happened.
I just got out, that's all.
So here I can say it's my grandfather, but it's also Hans Schorr.
Now, to top this off, many, many years later, after the war, we had a reunion of the people that we knew in Kanada.
There were, I think, five or six of us.
And I took them out for dinner at the Dan Hotel in Tel Aviv.
And we were sitting there.
And if a stranger would have walked by, they would have said to themselves, all this laughing.
They must be talking about beautiful things that happened yesterday or in their lives.
Because we were-- we were making jokes about things that happened.
There are funny things that happened, too, in camp.
And I said something.
I said, you know, I want to tell you something.
The-- the guy died, uh-- this Hans Schorr got killed trying to escape in 1944.
He was in Ohrdruf with me, which we're also going to come to.
And he tried to escape, they shot him.
And I saw it-- he and another fella.
And I said, you know, I thought about something a long time ago.
I said, if I walk down the street in Toronto, and I see Hans Schorr on the other side, what shall I do?
Kill him, or kiss him?
And you know what the consensus was of all of them?
You have to kiss him because he saved your life.
And it's funny.
Did you have any religious faith growing up?
Uh, well, yes and no.
I knew I was Jewish.
You know, I think that you, uh-- say religious faith, what-- I-- I-- my mother forced me to go to a cheder, and I learned nothing down there, as far as I was concerned.
I think I learned more about, uh, not being Jewish, but about Judaism in school.

Because the curriculum was such in Vienna that you-- that a, a teacher that was hired by the, by the [GERMAN], which was like the Jewish Congress here, came in once a week and took all the Jews from one level-- you know, like level three, level four, level five, you know, something like that-- put them all into one class and introduced them, instructed them in the Bible and holidays and Judaism, in general.
Uh-- and we were very assimilated, you know.
But-- but-- but there's one thing that I'm going to tell you.
If-- if you talk about beliefs-- I was a very tough kid and was a very rowdy kid sometimes.
And there was so much anti-Semitism.
